title: Add refunds endpoint and customer transaction history
summary: The PR introduces two new endpoints and a refunds service, but has several serious issues that must be fixed before merging. The most critical are: (1) a potential PCI leak exposing a raw card_number field in the history response, (2) a module-level global refunds store that bleeds state across test runs and app instances, (3) a broken sort that silently no-ops because it references a non-existent snake_case field on the Transaction domain model, and (4) a silent refund-amount-capping heuristic that masks bad client input instead of rejecting it. A handful of correctness, quality, and test issues round out the review.

findings.title: Possible raw card number leaked in API response
findings.message: The line `card_number: (tx as unknown as { card_number?: string }).card_number ?? tx.cardLast4` will emit whatever is sitting in a `card_number` property on the stored transaction object. If any upstream code (or a future persistence layer) ever attaches the full PAN to the record, this endpoint will broadcast it. Even today the double cast is a code smell that bypasses the type system to reach for data that should not exist on the wire. Remove the `card_number` field from the response entirely; `card_last4` is already present two lines below and that is the only safe value to expose.

findings.title: Module-level singleton refundsStore causes test pollution and shared state
findings.message: `const refundsStore: Record<string, RefundResult> = {}` is a module-level singleton. Every call to `createApp()` in tests shares the same store, so refunds created in one test case will be visible in later ones. This already affects the test suite: `POST /refunds` test 1 creates a refund, and any subsequent test that inspects refunds for the same transaction will see it. In production a hot-module-reload or multiple concurrent workers importing the module would also share state. Move `refundsStore` inside the `RefundsService` instance (as a `private readonly store: Map<string, RefundResult> = new Map()`) to match the pattern used by `InMemoryTransactionRepository`.

findings.title: Sort compares undefined fields — newest-first ordering is silently broken
findings.message: The comparator references `a.created_at` and `b.created_at`, but the `Transaction` domain model uses camelCase `createdAt`. Both sides are always `undefined`, so the comparator always returns 0 and the array is never reordered. The endpoint silently returns transactions in insertion order. Fix: sort on `a.createdAt` vs `b.createdAt`, or use `new Date(b.createdAt).getTime() - new Date(a.createdAt).getTime()`. Also remove the `: any` casts — they hid this bug.

findings.title: Silent refund-amount capping and unreliable unit-conversion heuristic
findings.message: When `refundAmount > tx.amount` the code tries to guess whether the client sent dollars instead of cents, and if the guess still fails it silently caps the refund to the full transaction amount. This has two problems: (a) a client sending a legitimately invalid amount (e.g., off-by-one) gets silently refunded the full amount rather than receiving an error, which is a financial correctness bug; (b) the heuristic (`refundAmount * 100 <= tx.amount`) is completely undocumented and will misfire on edge cases. The correct fix is to validate the amount at the schema layer (`z.number().positive().int()` if amounts are always in cents) and reject invalid requests with a 422, not silently mutate the amount.

findings.title: Transaction not found returns 200 instead of 404
findings.message: When `this.refunds.refund()` returns `null` (transaction does not exist), the controller responds with `200 {}`. Callers have no way to distinguish a successful no-op from a not-found error. This will cause silent failures in clients that check for a refund ID. Return `404` with a structured error body (e.g., `{ error: 'transaction_not_found' }`) instead.

findings.title: Injected PaymentsService does not share transactionRepo with RefundsService
findings.message: When `deps.payments` is provided (e.g., in integration tests that inject a custom service), the local `transactionRepo` is never used by `payments`; charges go into the injected service's own repo. But `refundsService` and `historyController` still hold a reference to the local `transactionRepo`, which is empty. Refunding a transaction created through the injected service will always return null. Either thread `transactionRepo` through `AppDeps` so callers can inject it, or document clearly that `deps.payments` must not be used together with refunds/history features.

findings.title: O(N) full table scan + N+1 refund lookups per request
findings.message: `findAll()` loads every transaction across all customers into memory, then filters in a loop. For each of the customer's transactions, `enrichTransactionWithRefunds` is called, which itself does a full O(M) scan of the global refunds store. The total cost is O(N + T*M) where N is total transactions and M is total refunds. This will degrade badly as data grows. Short term: add a `findByCustomerId(id)` method to the repository interface to avoid loading unrelated data. Longer term: index refunds by `transactionId`.

findings.title: Loose equality used for customerId comparison
findings.message: `tx.customerId == customerId` uses `==` instead of `===`. While both are strings in practice, the loose equality is inconsistent with the rest of the codebase and can hide type coercions (e.g., numeric IDs). Use strict equality `===`.

findings.title: amount field accepts zero and negative values
findings.message: `amount: z.number().optional()` allows zero and negative numbers, which would create refunds of 0 or negative amounts. Add `.positive()` and consider `.int()` if amounts are always in minor currency units: `z.number().int().positive().optional()`.

findings.title: No cumulative over-refund guard across multiple partial refunds
findings.message: The code checks that a single refund does not exceed `tx.amount`, but there is no check that the sum of all refunds for a transaction stays within `tx.amount`. A caller can refund 50% twice and end up refunding 100% + 100% of the original charge. Before creating a new refund, compute `listRefundsForTransaction(transactionId)`, sum existing refund amounts, and reject or cap if `existingTotal + newAmount > tx.amount`.

findings.title: historyQuerySchema is defined but never used
findings.message: `historyQuerySchema` is exported but not imported or used anywhere in the codebase. The `GET /customers/:id/transactions` route reads `req.params.id` directly without validation. Either apply the schema in the route handler (validating that `:id` is a non-empty string) or delete the dead export.

findings.title: Test assertions are too weak to catch regressions
findings.message: Three of the four assertions are effectively no-ops: `expect(refund.body).toBeDefined()` always passes (Express always sends a body), `expect(refund.status).toBeLessThan(500)` accepts 404 or 400 as a passing result, and `expect(res.body).toBeDefined()` in the history test is the same. These tests would pass even if the endpoints returned empty 200 responses with no data. At minimum assert: (1) `refund.status` is `201`, (2) `refund.body.refund_id` matches `rf_*`, (3) `refund.body.transaction_id` equals the charge id, (4) the history test should first charge and then assert the transaction appears in the list.

findings.title: console.log used instead of the pino structured logger
findings.message: The rest of the app uses `pino-http` for structured logging. Using `console.log` here (and in `refundsService.ts`) bypasses log level filtering, request correlation, and JSON formatting. Inject or import the pino logger instance and call `logger.info({ customerId }, 'history requested')` so the customer ID is a structured field rather than concatenated into the message string.

findings.title: reason parameter accepted but silently discarded
findings.message: The `refund` method accepts a `_reason` parameter (the leading underscore signals it is intentionally unused) but the value is never stored. If this field is part of the schema and returned to callers in the future, there will be no audit trail. Either store `reason` in `RefundResult` now, or remove it from the schema and the function signature until it is actually implemented.
